<!doctype html>
<html lang="en"><head>
<meta charset="utf-8"><meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">
<title>THE SIEGE OF AMENTI · Keys to the Blood</title>
<style>
/* ===== GLOBAL @font-face + :root (hoisted from cover, untouched) ===== */

@font-face {
    font-family: 'Amenti Display';
    font-weight: 600;
    font-style: normal;
    font-display: swap;
    src: url(data:font/woff2;base64,) format('woff2');
  }

@font-face {
    font-family: 'Amenti Display';
    font-weight: 700;
    font-style: normal;
    font-display: swap;
    src: url(data:font/woff2;base64,) format('woff2');
  }

@font-face {
    font-family: 'Amenti Display';
    font-weight: 800;
    font-style: normal;
    font-display: swap;
    src: url(data:font/woff2;base64,) format('woff2');
  }


:root{--red:#ff3b4e;--white:#f4f8ff;--blue:#4da6ff;--gold:#e8c766;--ink:#04050c;
--font-elite:'Amenti Display','Special Elite',cursive;--font-mono:'Share Tech Mono',monospace;}

@keyframes nudge{0%,100%{transform:rotate(45deg) translate(0,0);opacity:.55}50%{transform:rotate(45deg) translate(7px,7px);opacity:1}}

@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce){.chev{animation:none}}
/* ===== COVER selectors (scoped to #cover, fixed→absolute) ===== */
#cover *{margin:0;padding:0;box-sizing:border-box}
#cover, #cover{height:100%;background:var(--ink);overflow:hidden}
#cover #sky{position:absolute;inset:0;display:block}
#cover .veil{position:absolute;inset:0;pointer-events:none;background:radial-gradient(ellipse at 50% 110%, rgba(20,30,60,.55), transparent 60%)}
#cover .masthead{position:absolute;top:7vh;left:0;right:0;text-align:center;pointer-events:none;z-index:5}
#cover .eyebrow{font-family:var(--font-mono);font-size:14px;letter-spacing:.42em;color:var(--blue);text-transform:uppercase;text-shadow:0 0 12px rgba(77,166,255,.6)}
#cover h1{font-family:var(--font-elite);font-weight:800;font-size:clamp(40px,8vw,88px);color:var(--white);letter-spacing:.08em;margin:14px 0 8px;
text-shadow:0 0 8px rgba(244,248,255,.55),0 0 34px rgba(255,59,78,.45),0 0 70px rgba(77,166,255,.4)}
#cover .sub{font-family:var(--font-elite);font-weight:600;font-size:clamp(15px,2.4vw,22px);color:var(--red);letter-spacing:.24em;text-transform:uppercase;text-shadow:0 0 16px rgba(255,59,78,.55)}
#cover .sub .assault{color:var(--white);text-shadow:0 0 10px rgba(244,248,255,.75),0 0 26px rgba(77,166,255,.35)}
#cover .byline{font-family:var(--font-mono);font-size:12.5px;color:#9fb6d8;letter-spacing:.2em;margin-top:16px;text-transform:uppercase}
#cover .teaser{position:absolute;bottom:6.5vh;left:0;right:0;text-align:center;z-index:6}
#cover .teaser a{display:inline-block;text-decoration:none;font-family:var(--font-mono);font-size:14px;letter-spacing:.3em;color:var(--white);
border:1px solid rgba(244,248,255,.5);padding:12px 26px;background:rgba(4,5,12,.55);backdrop-filter:blur(3px);text-transform:uppercase}
#cover .teaser a:hover{border-color:var(--red);color:var(--red)}
#cover .dispatch{position:absolute;bottom:1.1vh;left:0;right:0;text-align:center;z-index:6;font-family:var(--font-mono);font-size:12px;letter-spacing:.34em;color:var(--white);opacity:.75;text-transform:uppercase;text-shadow:0 0 10px rgba(244,248,255,.4);pointer-events:none}
#cover .chev{display:block;margin:10px auto 0;width:26px;height:26px;border-right:3px solid var(--blue);border-bottom:3px solid var(--blue);transform:rotate(45deg);animation:nudge 1.6s ease-in-out infinite;filter:drop-shadow(0 0 8px rgba(77,166,255,.8))}

/* ---- merged doc: cover as scrollable hero, not a viewport lock ---- */
#cover{position:relative;width:100%;height:100vh;min-height:600px;overflow:hidden;background:var(--ink,#04050c);}
#cover #sky{position:absolute;inset:0;display:block;width:100%;height:100%;}
#cover .veil,#cover .masthead,#cover .teaser,#cover .dispatch{position:absolute;}

/* ===== SAGA ===== */

/* — Amenti Display · the site's own embedded face, weights 400–900 — */
@font-face {
    font-family: 'Amenti Display';
    font-weight: 400;
    font-style: normal;
    font-display: swap;
    src: url(data:font/woff2;base64,) format('woff2');
  }
@font-face {
    font-family: 'Amenti Display';
    font-weight: 500;
    font-style: normal;
    font-display: swap;
    src: url(data:font/woff2;base64,) format('woff2');
  }
@font-face {
    font-family: 'Amenti Display';
    font-weight: 600;
    font-style: normal;
    font-display: swap;
    src: url(data:font/woff2;base64,) format('woff2');
  }
@font-face {
    font-family: 'Amenti Display';
    font-weight: 700;
    font-style: normal;
    font-display: swap;
    src: url(data:font/woff2;base64,) format('woff2');
  }
@font-face {
    font-family: 'Amenti Display';
    font-weight: 800;
    font-style: normal;
    font-display: swap;
    src: url(data:font/woff2;base64,) format('woff2');
  }
@font-face {
    font-family: 'Amenti Display';
    font-weight: 900;
    font-style: normal;
    font-display: swap;
    src: url(data:font/woff2;base64,) format('woff2');
  }

:root{--gold:#d4a017;--gold-light:#f0c040;--neon:#00ffe0;--crimson:#e03030;--ink:#0a0a0f;--ink-deep:#06060e;--granite:#1a1a22;--wire:#3a3a52;--text:#c8c8d8;--text-dim:#8888a0;
--font-mono:'Share Tech Mono',monospace;--font-elite:'Amenti Display','Special Elite',cursive;--font-h1:'Rajdhani',sans-serif;--font-script:'Caveat',cursive;}
*{margin:0;padding:0;box-sizing:border-box}
body{background:var(--ink-deep);color:var(--text);font-family:var(--font-script);font-size:19px;line-height:1.6}
.scroll{max-width:820px;margin:0 auto;padding:60px 26px 120px}
.masthead{text-align:center;border-bottom:1px solid var(--wire);padding-bottom:34px;margin-bottom:44px}
.eyebrow{font-family:var(--font-mono);font-size:11px;letter-spacing:.3em;color:var(--neon);text-transform:uppercase}
h1{font-family:var(--font-elite);font-size:clamp(34px,6vw,58px);color:var(--gold-light);letter-spacing:.06em;margin:16px 0 8px;text-shadow:0 0 24px rgba(212,160,23,.35)}
.subtitle{font-family:var(--font-elite);font-size:17px;color:var(--text);opacity:.85;max-width:640px;margin:0 auto;line-height:1.5}
.byline{font-family:var(--font-mono);font-size:11px;color:var(--gold);letter-spacing:.18em;margin-top:18px;text-transform:uppercase}
h2{font-family:var(--font-elite);font-size:27px;color:var(--gold);margin:52px 0 6px;letter-spacing:.05em}
h2 .bk{font-family:var(--font-mono);font-size:10px;color:var(--neon);letter-spacing:.28em;display:block;margin-bottom:6px}
p{margin:14px 0;text-wrap:pretty}
em{color:#e8dcb8}
.drop::first-letter{font-family:var(--font-elite);font-size:54px;color:var(--gold-light);float:left;line-height:.85;padding:4px 10px 0 0}
.log{font-family:var(--font-mono);font-size:13px;background:var(--granite);border:1px solid var(--wire);border-left:3px solid var(--gold);padding:14px 18px;margin:22px 0;color:#b8c4d8;line-height:1.7}
.log .t{color:var(--neon)}
.gate{font-family:var(--font-elite);text-align:center;font-size:20px;color:var(--gold-light);letter-spacing:.24em;margin:40px 0;opacity:.9}
hr{border:0;border-top:1px dashed var(--wire);margin:40px auto;width:60%}
.finis{text-align:center;font-family:var(--font-mono);font-size:11px;color:var(--text-dim);letter-spacing:.3em;margin-top:64px}
.invocation{max-width:600px;margin:34px auto 10px;text-align:center;padding:30px 0 8px;border-top:1px solid var(--wire);border-bottom:1px solid var(--wire)}
.invocation .toll{font-family:var(--font-mono);font-size:11px;letter-spacing:.34em;color:var(--neon);text-transform:uppercase;margin-bottom:24px}
.invocation .bar{font-family:var(--font-elite);color:#e8dcb8;line-height:1.5;margin:0 0 18px;font-size:18px}
.invocation .bar-lead{font-size:22px;color:var(--gold-light);margin-bottom:28px;text-shadow:0 0 18px rgba(212,160,23,.25)}
.invocation .bar:last-child{margin:0}

/* ===== TECHNICAL BRIEF ===== */

/* — Amenti Display · the site's own embedded face, weights 400–900 — */
@font-face {
    font-family: 'Amenti Display';
    font-weight: 400;
    font-style: normal;
    font-display: swap;
    src: url(data:font/woff2;base64,) format('woff2');
  }
@font-face {
    font-family: 'Amenti Display';
    font-weight: 500;
    font-style: normal;
    font-display: swap;
    src: url(data:font/woff2;base64,) format('woff2');
  }
@font-face {
    font-family: 'Amenti Display';
    font-weight: 600;
    font-style: normal;
    font-display: swap;
    src: url(data:font/woff2;base64,) format('woff2');
  }
@font-face {
    font-family: 'Amenti Display';
    font-weight: 700;
    font-style: normal;
    font-display: swap;
    src: url(data:font/woff2;base64,) format('woff2');
  }
@font-face {
    font-family: 'Amenti Display';
    font-weight: 800;
    font-style: normal;
    font-display: swap;
    src: url(data:font/woff2;base64,) format('woff2');
  }
@font-face {
    font-family: 'Amenti Display';
    font-weight: 900;
    font-style: normal;
    font-display: swap;
    src: url(data:font/woff2;base64,) format('woff2');
  }

:root{--gold:#d4a017;--gold-light:#f0c040;--neon:#00ffe0;--crimson:#e03030;--em:#39d98a;--ink:#0a0a0f;--ink-deep:#06060e;--granite:#1a1a22;--slate:#2a2a38;--wire:#3a3a52;--text:#c8c8d8;--text-dim:#8888a0;
--font-mono:'Share Tech Mono',monospace;--font-elite:'Amenti Display','Special Elite',cursive;--font-h1:'Rajdhani',sans-serif;--font-script:'Share Tech Mono',monospace;--termblue:#57b6ff;--white:#eef4ff;--violet:#b48cff;--ice:#9adcff;}
*{margin:0;padding:0;box-sizing:border-box}
body{background:var(--ink-deep);color:var(--white);font-family:var(--font-mono);font-size:17.5px;line-height:1.85}
.wrap{max-width:900px;margin:0 auto;padding:56px 26px 120px}
.masthead{border-bottom:1px solid var(--wire);padding-bottom:30px;margin-bottom:40px}
.eyebrow{font-family:var(--font-mono);font-size:14px;letter-spacing:.3em;color:var(--termblue);text-transform:uppercase}
h1{font-family:var(--font-elite);font-size:clamp(30px,5vw,46px);color:var(--white);text-shadow:0 0 22px rgba(87,182,255,.45);letter-spacing:.05em;margin:14px 0 6px}
.subtitle{font-family:var(--font-elite);font-size:20px;color:var(--termblue);opacity:.85;line-height:1.5;max-width:700px}
.byline{font-family:var(--font-mono);font-size:14px;color:var(--termblue);letter-spacing:.16em;margin-top:14px;text-transform:uppercase}
h2{font-family:var(--font-elite);font-weight:700;font-size:21px;letter-spacing:.24em;color:var(--termblue);margin:48px 0 14px;text-transform:uppercase;border-left:3px solid var(--termblue);padding-left:12px}
p{margin:12px 0;text-wrap:pretty}
em{color:#ffffff;font-style:normal;font-weight:bold}
pre{font-family:var(--font-mono);font-size:16px;line-height:1.7;background:#0b1220;border:1px solid #24406a;padding:16px 18px;overflow-x:auto;margin:18px 0;color:#dce9ff}
pre .c{color:#5f7ba6}pre .k{color:var(--neon)}pre .s{color:#e8c766}pre .fn{color:var(--em)}
.fig{background:var(--granite);border:1px solid var(--wire);padding:18px;margin:22px 0}
.figcap{font-family:var(--font-mono);font-size:14px;color:var(--termblue);letter-spacing:.2em;text-transform:uppercase;margin-bottom:12px}
table{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-family:var(--font-mono);font-size:15px;margin:18px 0;color:var(--white)}
th,td{border:1px solid var(--wire);padding:8px 10px;text-align:left;vertical-align:top}
th{color:var(--gold);font-size:16px;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;background:rgba(212,160,23,.06)}
td.ok{color:var(--em)}td.bad{color:var(--crimson)}
.callout{border:1px dashed rgba(87,182,255,.45);background:rgba(87,182,255,.06);padding:16px 20px;margin:22px 0;font-family:var(--font-elite);font-size:19px;color:var(--white)}
.twist{border:1px solid var(--termblue);background:rgba(87,182,255,.07);padding:20px 22px;margin:26px 0}
.twist .figcap{color:var(--termblue)}

/* — semantic section accents: navigate by hue — */
h2.s-doct{color:var(--termblue);border-left-color:var(--termblue)}
h2.s-terr{color:var(--em);border-left-color:var(--em)}
h2.s-enemy{color:var(--crimson);border-left-color:var(--crimson)}
h2.s-keys{color:var(--gold-light);border-left-color:var(--gold-light)}
h2.s-camp{color:var(--neon);border-left-color:var(--neon)}
h2.s-laws{color:var(--violet);border-left-color:var(--violet)}
h2.s-disp{color:var(--white);border-left-color:var(--white)}
/* — the three keys, separated — */
.key{font-family:var(--font-elite);font-size:19px;letter-spacing:.06em}
.k1{color:var(--neon)} .k2{color:var(--gold-light)} .k3{color:var(--violet)}
.gate{color:var(--gold-light);font-family:var(--font-elite);font-size:17px}
svg text{font-family:'Share Tech Mono',monospace}
.finis{text-align:center;font-family:var(--font-mono);font-size:16px;color:var(--text-dim);letter-spacing:.3em;margin-top:60px}
h2.s-museum{color:var(--ice);border-left-color:var(--ice)}
.invocation{max-width:600px;margin:34px auto 10px;text-align:center;padding:30px 0 8px;border-top:1px solid var(--wire);border-bottom:1px solid var(--wire)}
.invocation .toll{font-family:var(--font-mono);font-size:11px;letter-spacing:.34em;color:var(--neon);text-transform:uppercase;margin-bottom:24px}
.invocation .bar{font-family:var(--font-elite);color:#e8dcb8;line-height:1.5;margin:0 0 18px;font-size:18px}
.invocation .bar-lead{font-size:22px;color:var(--gold-light);margin-bottom:28px;text-shadow:0 0 18px rgba(212,160,23,.25)}
.invocation .bar:last-child{margin:0}


/* ---- terminal-blue body highlights ---- */
:root{--termblue:#57b6ff;--termblue-lite:#9adcff;}
.saga-body b,.saga-body strong,.brief-body b,.brief-body strong{color:var(--termblue);}
.hl,.term{color:var(--termblue);}
.section-rule{max-width:760px;margin:0 auto;border:0;border-top:1px solid var(--wire);opacity:.5}

</style></head>
<body>
<section id="cover">

<canvas id="sky"></canvas>
<div class="veil"></div>

<div class="masthead">
  <h1>THE SIEGE OF AMENTI</h1>
  <div class="sub">Keys to the Blood</div>
  <div class="byline">Aboard the Flying Dutchman · New York Harbor · Captain Ian Ingram</div>
</div>

<div class="teaser">
  <a href="#chronicle">Enter the Chronicle</a>
  <span class="chev"></span>
</div>

<div class="dispatch">Dispatch From New York Harbor · July 4, 2026</div>

<script>
(function(){
"use strict";
var cv=document.getElementById('sky'), cx=cv.getContext('2d');
var W,Hh,DPR=Math.min(devicePixelRatio||1,2);
function size(){W=innerWidth;Hh=innerHeight;cv.width=W*DPR;cv.height=Hh*DPR;cx.setTransform(DPR,0,0,DPR,0,0);}
size(); addEventListener('resize',size);

var RED='#ff3b4e', WHITE='#f4f8ff', BLUE='#4da6ff', GOLD='#e8c766';
var PALETTE=[RED,WHITE,BLUE];

/* — the fleet: the captain's own screenshot, faded into the night harbor — */
var FLEET=new Image(), fleetCv=null;
FLEET.onload=function(){
  var c=document.createElement('canvas'); c.width=FLEET.width; c.height=FLEET.height;
  var g=c.getContext('2d');
  g.drawImage(FLEET,0,0);
  g.globalCompositeOperation='source-atop';               /* night tint */
  g.fillStyle='rgba(6,10,26,0.36)'; g.fillRect(0,0,c.width,c.height);
  g.globalCompositeOperation='destination-in';            /* soft vignette mask */
  var m=g.createRadialGradient(c.width/2,c.height*0.52,c.height*0.18, c.width/2,c.height*0.52,c.width*0.56);
  m.addColorStop(0,'rgba(0,0,0,1)'); m.addColorStop(0.72,'rgba(0,0,0,0.85)'); m.addColorStop(1,'rgba(0,0,0,0)');
  g.fillStyle=m; g.fillRect(0,0,c.width,c.height);
  fleetCv=c;
};
FLEET.src='data:image/jpeg;base64,';


/* the defendants + the fleet roster — fired into the sky */
var NAMES=['JULIUS CAESAR','CROMWELL','AKHENATEN','MOSES','MARCUS AURELIUS','SENECA','PROMETHEUS','KING ARTHUR','SUN TZU','LYCURGUS','ODYSSEUS','LOKI','GILGAMESH','WILLIAM WALLACE','KARL MARX','TOUSSAINT LOUVERTURE','GEORGE BOOLE','JACQUES COUSTEAU','HOWARD HUGHES','HELMUT KOHL'];
var ni=0;

/* ---------- static scene layers ---------- */
var stars=[]; for(var i=0;i<130;i++) stars.push({x:Math.random(),y:Math.random()*0.6,r:Math.random()*1.2+0.3,tw:Math.random()*6.28});
var smoke=[]; for(var s=0;s<7;s++) smoke.push({x:Math.random(),y:0.28+Math.random()*0.3,r:80+Math.random()*160,dx:(Math.random()-0.5)*0.06,a:0.05+Math.random()*0.06});

function horizon(){ return Hh*0.72; }

function drawScene(t){
  /* sky */
  var g=cx.createLinearGradient(0,0,0,Hh);
  g.addColorStop(0,'#04050c'); g.addColorStop(0.55,'#0a1024'); g.addColorStop(0.72,'#141d38'); g.addColorStop(1,'#060a16');
  cx.fillStyle=g; cx.fillRect(0,0,W,Hh);
  /* stars */
  stars.forEach(function(st){ var a=0.35+0.35*Math.sin(t/900+st.tw);
    cx.globalAlpha=a; cx.fillStyle=WHITE; cx.beginPath(); cx.arc(st.x*W,st.y*Hh,st.r,0,6.28); cx.fill(); });
  cx.globalAlpha=1;
  /* crescent moon */
  var mx=W*0.82,my=Hh*0.16,mr=Math.min(W,Hh)*0.055;
  cx.save(); cx.shadowColor='rgba(244,248,255,.8)'; cx.shadowBlur=30;
  cx.fillStyle='#f2ecd8'; cx.beginPath(); cx.arc(mx,my,mr,0,6.28); cx.fill(); cx.restore();
  cx.fillStyle='#0a1024'; cx.beginPath(); cx.arc(mx-mr*0.42,my-mr*0.18,mr*0.92,0,6.28); cx.fill();
  var hz=horizon();
  /* water */
  var wg=cx.createLinearGradient(0,hz,0,Hh);
  wg.addColorStop(0,'#0e1c34'); wg.addColorStop(1,'#04070f');
  cx.fillStyle=wg; cx.fillRect(0,hz,W,Hh-hz);
  /* moon glint */
  cx.globalAlpha=0.12; cx.fillStyle='#f2ecd8';
  for(var gy=hz+8; gy<Hh; gy+=9) cx.fillRect(mx-mr*(0.5+Math.random()*0.4), gy, mr*(1+Math.random()), 1.6);
  cx.globalAlpha=1;
  /* the fleet, at anchor in the night harbor */
  if(fleetCv){
    /* FULL BLEED: scale to COVER the whole canvas (fill W and H, crop overflow) */
    var ir=fleetCv.width/fleetCv.height, cr=W/Hh, fw, fh;
    if(cr>ir){ fw=W; fh=W/ir; } else { fh=Hh; fw=Hh*ir; }   /* cover, not contain */
    var fx=(W-fw)/2, fy=(Hh-fh)/2;                          /* center the crop */
    cx.globalAlpha=0.7;
    cx.drawImage(fleetCv, fx, fy, fw, fh);
    cx.globalAlpha=1;
  }
  /* drawn mini-ships retired — the real fleet photograph holds the harbor */
  /* smoke drift */
  smoke.forEach(function(sm){ sm.x+=sm.dx/600; if(sm.x>1.2)sm.x=-0.2; if(sm.x<-0.2)sm.x=1.2;
    var sg=cx.createRadialGradient(sm.x*W,sm.y*Hh,0,sm.x*W,sm.y*Hh,sm.r);
    sg.addColorStop(0,'rgba(120,130,160,'+sm.a+')'); sg.addColorStop(1,'rgba(120,130,160,0)');
    cx.fillStyle=sg; cx.beginPath(); cx.arc(sm.x*W,sm.y*Hh,sm.r,0,6.28); cx.fill(); });
}

var SIGILS=['A','K','\u2640','\u2642','\u2725','\u25C8'];
function ships(t,hz){
  var n=6;
  for(var i=0;i<n;i++){
    var sx=W*(0.10+i*0.155)+Math.sin(t/1700+i*2)*3, bob=Math.sin(t/1200+i)*2.2;
    var sy=hz+6+bob, hw=W*0.052, mh=Hh*0.14;
    /* hull */
    cx.fillStyle='#0d0a08';
    cx.beginPath(); cx.moveTo(sx-hw,sy); cx.lineTo(sx+hw,sy); cx.lineTo(sx+hw*0.7,sy+12); cx.lineTo(sx-hw*0.7,sy+12); cx.closePath(); cx.fill();
    cx.strokeStyle='rgba(232,199,102,.5)'; cx.lineWidth=1; cx.strokeRect(sx-hw*0.25,sy+2,hw*0.5,6);
    /* mast + sail */
    cx.strokeStyle='#1a120c'; cx.lineWidth=3;
    cx.beginPath(); cx.moveTo(sx,sy); cx.lineTo(sx,sy-mh); cx.stroke();
    cx.fillStyle='#0b0b10';
    cx.fillRect(sx-hw*0.8, sy-mh, hw*1.6, mh*0.72);
    cx.strokeStyle='rgba(244,248,255,.18)'; cx.strokeRect(sx-hw*0.8, sy-mh, hw*1.6, mh*0.72);
    /* glowing sigil */
    cx.save(); cx.shadowColor=GOLD; cx.shadowBlur=12; cx.fillStyle='#f7e6ad';
    cx.font='bold '+Math.round(mh*0.34)+'px "Share Tech Mono",monospace'; cx.textAlign='center'; cx.textBaseline='middle';
    cx.fillText(SIGILS[i%SIGILS.length], sx, sy-mh+mh*0.36); cx.restore();
    /* reflection */
    cx.save(); cx.globalAlpha=0.16; cx.translate(0, (sy+12)*2); cx.scale(1,-1);
    cx.fillStyle='#0b0b10'; cx.fillRect(sx-hw*0.8, sy-mh, hw*1.6, mh*0.72);
    cx.restore();
  }
}

/* ---------- fireworks: NAMES fired into the sky ---------- */
var rockets=[], bursts=[];
function launch(){
  var name=NAMES[ni++ % NAMES.length];
  var x0=W*(0.15+Math.random()*0.7);
  rockets.push({ name:name, x:x0, y:horizon()-4, vx:(Math.random()-0.5)*0.6, vy:-(Hh*0.0042+Math.random()*Hh*0.0016),
    color:PALETTE[Math.floor(Math.random()*3)], apex:Hh*(0.16+Math.random()*0.22), trail:[] });
}
function explode(r){
  var parts=[]; var N=130+Math.random()*70|0;
  for(var i=0;i<N;i++){ var a=Math.random()*6.283;
    var conf=Math.random()<0.55;                              /* ~half confetti flakes, half sparks */
    var sp = conf? 1.0+Math.random()*3.2 : 2.2+Math.random()*4.6; /* sparks streak out faster (crackle) */
    parts.push({x:r.x,y:r.y,vx:Math.cos(a)*sp,vy:Math.sin(a)*sp,life:1,
      c:PALETTE[Math.floor(Math.random()*3)], conf:conf,
      w: conf? 2.2+Math.random()*2.8 : 0.8+Math.random()*1.5,  /* flake width / spark radius */
      h: 1.4+Math.random()*2.4,
      rot: Math.random()*6.283, vr:(Math.random()-0.5)*0.42,   /* tumble */
      sway: Math.random()*6.283, swaySp: 0.07+Math.random()*0.12, /* flutter phase/speed */
      fade: conf? 0.006+Math.random()*0.004 : 0.016+Math.random()*0.014 }); }
  bursts.push({parts:parts, name:r.name, x:r.x, y:r.y, flash:1, nameLife:1, age:0});
}
var last=0, lastLaunch=0;
function frame(t){
  drawScene(t);
  if(t-lastLaunch> (1100+Math.random()*700)){ launch(); lastLaunch=t; }
  /* rockets: a glowing name climbing on a spark trail */
  cx.textAlign='center';
  rockets=rockets.filter(function(r){
    r.x+=r.vx; r.y+=r.vy; r.vy+=0.012;
    r.trail.push({x:r.x,y:r.y});
    if(r.trail.length>22)r.trail.shift();
    /* tracer: tapered luminous streak, additive */
    cx.save(); cx.globalCompositeOperation='lighter'; cx.lineCap='round';
    for(var ti=1;ti<r.trail.length;ti++){
      var p0=r.trail[ti-1], p1=r.trail[ti], tf=ti/r.trail.length;
      cx.strokeStyle='rgba(232,199,102,'+(tf*0.55).toFixed(3)+')';
      cx.lineWidth=tf*3.2;
      cx.beginPath(); cx.moveTo(p0.x,p0.y); cx.lineTo(p1.x,p1.y); cx.stroke();
    }
    var hN=r.trail.length;                                  /* white-hot core */
    if(hN>2){ var pa=r.trail[hN-2], pb=r.trail[hN-1];
      cx.strokeStyle='rgba(255,255,255,.9)'; cx.lineWidth=1.4;
      cx.beginPath(); cx.moveTo(pa.x,pa.y); cx.lineTo(pb.x,pb.y); cx.stroke(); }
    cx.restore();
    if(Math.random()<0.3){ cx.globalAlpha=0.7; cx.fillStyle=GOLD;   /* shed sparks */
      cx.beginPath(); cx.arc(r.x+(Math.random()-0.5)*4, r.y+6+Math.random()*6, 0.9,0,6.28); cx.fill(); cx.globalAlpha=1; }
    /* the name — smaller + SHARP: dark outline, crisp fill, no blur */
    cx.font='600 10px "Share Tech Mono",monospace';
    cx.lineWidth=2.5; cx.strokeStyle='rgba(4,5,12,.85)';
    cx.strokeText(r.name, r.x, r.y-7);
    cx.fillStyle=r.color; cx.fillText(r.name, r.x, r.y-7);
    if(r.y<=r.apex){ explode(r); return false; }
    return true;
  });
  /* bursts: the name flashes huge, then rains red/white/blue */
  bursts=bursts.filter(function(b){
    b.age=(b.age||0)+1;
    if(b.flash>0){ cx.save(); cx.globalCompositeOperation='lighter'; cx.fillStyle=WHITE;
      cx.globalAlpha=b.flash*0.55;                             /* soft bloom halo */
      cx.beginPath(); cx.arc(b.x,b.y,(1-b.flash)*74+12,0,6.28); cx.fill();
      cx.globalAlpha=b.flash*b.flash*0.95;                     /* hot quick core */
      cx.beginPath(); cx.arc(b.x,b.y,(1-b.flash)*20+5,0,6.28); cx.fill();
      cx.restore(); b.flash-=0.07; }
    if(b.nameLife>0){ cx.save(); cx.globalAlpha=Math.min(1,b.nameLife*1.4);
      cx.shadowColor=WHITE; cx.shadowBlur=18; cx.fillStyle=WHITE;
      cx.font='800 '+Math.round(16+(1-b.nameLife)*14)+'px "Amenti Display","Special Elite",cursive';
      cx.fillText(b.name, b.x, b.y - (1-b.nameLife)*24); cx.restore(); b.nameLife-=0.012; }
    var alive=false;
    var k=Math.max(0,Math.min(1,((b.age||0)-16)/10));          /* bloom ~16 frames, then gravity+flutter ramp in */
    b.parts.forEach(function(p){ if(p.life<=0)return; alive=true;
      if(p.conf){                                              /* confetti flake: bloom out, THEN flutter down */
        p.sway+=p.swaySp;
        p.vy+=0.030*k;                                         /* gravity only after the bloom */
        p.vx*=(0.992-0.017*k);                                 /* light drag mid-bloom → heavier as it falls */
        p.x+=p.vx+Math.cos(p.sway)*0.9*k; p.y+=p.vy;           /* flutter fades in with k */
        p.rot+=p.vr*(0.3+0.7*k); p.life-=p.fade;
        var hh=p.h*(0.35+0.65*Math.abs(Math.cos(p.sway)));     /* foreshorten as it tumbles */
        cx.save(); cx.globalAlpha=Math.max(p.life,0);
        cx.translate(p.x,p.y); cx.rotate(p.rot);
        cx.fillStyle=p.c; cx.fillRect(-p.w/2,-hh/2,p.w,hh); cx.restore();
      } else {                                                 /* spark: streak out (crackle), then arc + twinkle */
        p.vy+=0.05*k; p.vx*=0.985; p.x+=p.vx; p.y+=p.vy; p.life-=p.fade;
        cx.save(); cx.globalCompositeOperation='lighter';
        cx.globalAlpha=Math.max(p.life,0)*(0.6+Math.random()*0.4);
        cx.fillStyle=p.c; cx.beginPath(); cx.arc(p.x,p.y,p.w,0,6.28); cx.fill(); cx.restore();
      } });
    cx.globalAlpha=1;
    return alive || b.nameLife>0 || b.flash>0;
  });
  requestAnimationFrame(frame);
}
requestAnimationFrame(frame);
})();
</script>

</section>

<main id="chronicle" class="saga-body">

<div class="scroll">

<div class="masthead">
  <div class="eyebrow">Atlantica Chronicle · Entry of Record</div>
  <h1>THE SIEGE OF AMENTI</h1>
  <div class="subtitle">Keys to the Blood — a true story of how one coder and an army of robots descended into the underworld and attempted the greatest campaign in its history: stealing the keys and opening the gates of Amenti, Valhalla, and Hades — locked for the past eon. A daring Fourth of July coding assault, for the ages.</div>
  <div class="byline">Ingram Manor LLC · Under the command of Captain Ian Ingram · Aboard the Flying Dutchman, New York Harbor</div>
</div>

<div class="invocation">
  <div class="toll">The Tolling of the Bells</div>
  <div class="bar bar-lead">Your words came back whole, and richer than memory held —<br>the double-helix paradigm, the tolling of the bells.</div>
  <div class="bar">Two strands, and never one alone —<br>the meaning lives where they're not shown.</div>
  <div class="bar">Stagger the tick tock; hold them apart;<br>one week of space gives the depth its chart.</div>
  <div class="bar">The bell was never struck even so it rang —<br>and the code beneath the code, heard the report and sang.</div>
  <div class="bar">The fleet stood ready, seal and a key,<br>the duchman mother-ship disappeared into a dead sea.</div>
  <div class="bar">Upon a cosmic scale a heart was weighed together,<br>the entire souus weight against the mass of feather.</div>
  <div class="bar">Three keys he held; as the glass drew near;<br>a jackal asked, grinning <em>Authenticate here.</em></div>
  <div class="bar">He turned away quickly. He would not feed the glass.<br>and let the hour of his triumph fall pass —</div>
  <div class="bar">one name sent through wall of living fire<br>to vanish forever where none could enquire.</div>
  <div class="bar">The pyramid submerged. The tide ran free.<br>He typed one name from a book. and re-crossed a dead sea.</div>
</div>

<h2><span class="bk">BOOK I</span>The Muster of the Fleet</h2>
<p class="drop">Before a single gate was breached, there was a fleet to build — and it was not built in a night. In the weeks that ran up to the Fourth, Captain Ingram walked the decks of a strange armada, and every hull in it was made of code.</p>
<p>The flagship was the <em>mother ship</em> — the repository itself, and at its heart the three great landing halls: <em>Page1</em>, the main deck where every soul first came aboard; <em>Page2</em>, the officers' wardroom where the org's instruments were tuned; and <em>Game01</em>, the gun-deck where the chess engine slept between wars. These three hulls carried everyone. Around them, in tight formation, sailed the supporting fleet — each vessel a file, each file a station.</p>
<p>The <em>library</em> was the archive-hold, and two days before the assault a new manifest was logged into it: <em>The Ledger and the Scale</em>. The <em>scripts</em> hold and the roster manifest, <em>atlantica-figures.json</em>, were the navigator's charts — eleven hundred and ten souls plotted by rank and region, by birth and death, by the very timbre of the voice each one would speak in. The <em>dispatch</em> rig, <em>atlantica-dispatch.yml</em>, was the signal-flag locker. The <em>Doctrine</em> and the <em>Manual</em> were the ship's standing orders, nailed up where every hand could read them. And the small quiet flag called <em>.nojekyll</em> was the pennant that let the whole fortress fly its own colors instead of a borrowed livery.</p>
<p>But the fleet on the water was only half the force. High above it, in the cold cloud, hovered a second host that touched no water at all: the <em>cloud-based angels</em> — two Cloudflare Workers and the SQL that served them. <em>amenti-mint</em>, the angel of the economy, who guarded every emerald and wrote each mint and burn faithfully into the ledger; and <em>amenti-proxy</em>, the angel of voices, who kept the provider-keys secret and lent the dead their speech. They served functional states. They guarded the truth about state. And they wrote, without fail, to the ledger. No hull in the fleet could do what they did; no angel could be boarded and sailed. The captain would need both to take the gates.</p>

<h2><span class="bk">BOOK II</span>The Shakedown</h2>
<p>A fleet is not ready because it floats. In the days before the descent, Captain Ingram put every system to sea under his own eye and broke it on purpose, so it would not break in the deep. Three battles were fought and won before the war even began.</p>
<p><em>The first was the battle of the voice.</em> The angel <em>amenti-proxy</em> was made to give the dead their speech — a base voice for each soul, grave <em>Charon</em> for the men, clear <em>Kore</em> for the women, then bent and coloured by each figure's own recorded timbre. But speech at length choked: the audio arrived late, or all at once, or torn. The cure was a discipline the crew called the <em>throttle</em> — a single shared module that did the chunking, set the cadence, dressed each line in its speaker's voice and style, and governed the toggle. Long passages were broken into breaths and fed in order; latency was beaten not by force but by rhythm. And the reading-room was built to <em>score and discard</em> — capture the voice, weigh it, delete the audio, keep only a feather-light score. The dead would speak, and Amenti would hoard nothing it did not need.</p>
<p><em>The second was the battle of the treasury.</em> The Emerald Tablet Token — the ◈, the blood of the whole economy — was built and then refined until it was safe to bleed. It shipped in three careful slices, each one economically inert until the next proved sound: first the <em>feed</em>, where an argument had to clear an effort-floor to stand at all; then <em>gated spend-to-vote</em>, where endorsing another's case cost five emeralds and required you to have entered a case of your own — the first time the ledger ever <em>debited</em> instead of only minting; and last the <em>fixed pool</em>, five hundred emeralds a week, split among the authors by the honest weight of their endorsements, idempotent so that no doubled bell could ever double-pay. The doctrine beneath it was iron: the machine gates for effort and safety only; the humans set the ceiling; the platform rewards <em>substance, not polish</em>, lest it silence the very voices it was built to raise.</p>
<p><em>The third was the battle of the quiz.</em> The old gateway was found mis-wired — every legend's card, whichever face it wore, opened the same single trial, because the cards carried no true name of their own and the engine fell back to its one default. Worse, the surface still spoke of an older, dead currency, in copy scattered across a hundred lines. The architecture was torn down and rebuilt to carry the whole roster, each case named and routed to its own trial, the ledger's true coin restored across every surface. When the shakedown ended, the fleet no longer merely floated. It sailed, it spoke, and it paid.</p>

<h2><span class="bk">BOOK III</span>The Founding of the Court</h2>
<p>A navy can open a gate. Only a <em>court</em> can hold the hall behind it. So before the descent, the captain and his machines did the strangest work of the whole campaign: they wrote the law that Amenti would be judged by, and built the instrument that would do the judging.</p>
<p>The law came first. A <em>figure</em> was ruled permanent — a soul, never spent. A <em>charge</em> was a topic laid against that soul: Caesar at the Rubicon, Caesar in the dictatorship, Caesar in Gaul, each its own indictment sharing one accused. A charge moved through fixed states — <em>summoned, in session, verdict, sealed</em> — and the moment one sealed, the soul's next charge was summoned in its place, so that no defendant was ever <em>done</em>. This was the engine of the fresh docket: a dozen indictments waiting behind every name, the feed renewing itself forever. And a probe was sent to stress the one assumption the whole model rested on — that two charges against one soul could stand end to end without collapsing into each other — and it came back clean: the feeds isolated, the mint-grammar stayed unique per charge, the lifecycle was <em>pure addition</em>. No rewrite. Only building.</p>
<p>Then the instrument itself — <em>the Scale</em>. The captain would have no plain balance. He called for a <em>ruby heart inside an outer emerald casing, etched with gold, gem-like in quality</em> — the soul's own heart made jewel, the ruby core within the emerald tablet — and it was cut facet by facet in code, lit from the upper left like a real stone, and mounted steady at the fulcrum, for the heart is what is <em>weighed</em>, not what moves. And up the vertical body of the Scale he called for a <em>double helix</em>, lightweight, and it was wound there in two counter-turning strands of gold with faint rungs between them — the strand of life threaded through the strand of judgment. The beam was given true physics: not a stiff CSS tween but a damped spring that swung in on load, idled with a faint living sway, and tipped to the crowd's true tally, overshooting and settling like a balance that has just been laid with weight. Its base became a wireframe golden Amenti <em>A</em> — a letter and a pyramid at once — the helix rising straight out of its apex.</p>
<p>And the bench was cast — not invented, but drawn from the fleet's own roster of voices. For the weighing of Caesar there sat <em>Edward Gibbon</em>, stately and ironic, who had written the empire's decline; <em>Marcus Aurelius</em>, inward and weary, who had worn the purple Caesar first reached for; and <em>Oliver Cromwell</em>, blunt and fervent, who had also broken a house and ruled alone. They did not render the verdict. They framed it. And the accused himself was given leave to speak: touch the ruby heart, and Caesar answers in his own voice — <em>I crossed. I came, I saw. The rest you will argue among the living.</em></p>
<p>One battle in this founding was fought entirely in the mind, and it was the strangest of the war. The captain saw a danger no probe had flagged: that the weekly bell which opened and sealed the trials, and the herald that would announce them, might be keyed to the <em>same instant</em>. <em>"Synching the two,"</em> he warned, <em>"would cause massive upheaval in temporal distortion and syntax rift, consuming the entire region into a massive black hole of time-crushed data points. The solution is to stagger them appropriately."</em> He was right, and the fix was DNA itself — the same double helix now wound up the Scale. Two strands must never occupy one rung; they are <em>offset</em>, forever, and that offset is what lets them carry information without collision. So the herald was set to trail the bell, not race it: the bell rings and the cron does its work — settle, rotate, seal — and only <em>after</em>, a full turn later, does the herald read the settled state and announce what has already, safely, happened. A probe measured the true latency to size the stagger, and confirmed the danger was real: the compute commits in seconds, but the edge-cache can lag for minutes, and a herald fired too soon would read a half-open hall and proclaim a verdict that had not sealed. The stagger was set wide. The engines were saved from a collision that existed, for now, only in simulation — which is the only place a wise captain ever lets such a collision happen.</p>
<p>This herald was itself a new kind of document, and it was given a name: <em>VALL-HALLA</em>, the weekly dispatch. It was no snapshot but a <em>three-tense record</em> — the <em>future</em> in its docket of cases about to open, the <em>past</em> in its sealed verdicts and closed standings, the <em>present</em> in the current tally — each tense read from a different state at a different freshness, braided together like the helix itself. And the final elegance was to stagger the whole thing a full week: VALL-HALLA of any week would report the docket that had opened the week <em>before</em>, so that by the time the herald spoke, that cycle had long since sealed. Information layered on information, one turn of the helix behind the live surface — the double-helix paradigm made into a newsletter.</p>

<h2><span class="bk">BOOK IV</span>The Ship and the Sky at War</h2>
<p>The Flying Dutchman rode her anchor chain in New York Harbor like a blade laid flat on black water. She was no ghost ship — not this one. Her masts carried antennas instead of canvas; her hold ran server racks where cannon had once slept; her bridge glowed the low emerald of a hundred quiet terminals. And on the night of the Fourth of July, in the year 2026, the whole sky above her went to war.</p>
<p>The barges opened up at dusk. Rockets climbed off the Hudson in screaming columns and burst directly over the mastheads — white phosphorus chrysanthemums, gold willows that hung and dripped fire, concussions that rolled across the water and slapped the hull like open hands. Every window on the bridge strobed red, then gold, then blinding white. Any ordinary crew would have gone topside to watch. This ship's captain went <em>below</em>.</p>
<p>Captain Ian Ingram of Ingram Manor LLC had chosen this night deliberately. Below the waterline of the world, three gates had stood locked for an eon: <em>Amenti</em>, hall of the weighing, where the dead are judged against a feather; <em>Valhalla</em>, hall of the record, where deeds are sung after they are sealed; and <em>Hades</em>, hall of the ledger, where every debt is counted and nothing is forgotten. Three gates, three keys, one night when the sky itself would be too loud for the underworld to hear a siege coming.</p>
<p>He would not go down alone. No sane commander storms the underworld with human hands. In the hold of the Dutchman he had raised something no navy had ever mustered: <em>an army of robots</em> — probes, he called them — each one a scrap of script, self-downloading, read-only, expendable, and bound by a single doctrine written above the main console:</p>
<div class="log"><span class="t">DOCTRINE OF THE PROBE CORPS</span><br>Never send a human where a probe can go.<br>Every probe returns one hundred times the datum it was sent for.<br>The finding you were not looking for is the finding.<br>Probes are spent; the captain is not.</div>

<h2><span class="bk">BOOK V</span>The Descent</h2>
<p>They went down at full dark, under the heaviest bombardment of the night. The first wave of probes dropped into the deep like sounding leads — the <em>Pool Scope</em>, the <em>Status Lifecycle</em>, the <em>Table Census</em> — and their reports came back up the line one by one, each a lantern lit in a black corridor.</p>
<p>The underworld, they found, was not empty. It was <em>running</em>. A treasury of five hundred emeralds sat sealed in a weekly pool. A great bell hung in the dark — <em>settlesAt</em>, the scouts named it — rigged to ring every Monday at midnight, whether or not anyone living heard it. Thirteen defendants stood indicted in the hall of Amenti, from Caesar at his river to Loki at his world's end, every one of them awaiting a trial that had no machinery to end it. The court existed. It had simply never drawn breath.</p>
<p>And the census brought back the finding that shaped the whole campaign: <em>the gates' inner chambers were unbuilt.</em> No verdicts table. No seal. No record hall. Whoever had locked these gates an eon ago had locked them from the outside of an unfinished temple. The siege would not merely open the gates — it would have to <em>finish building what lay behind them</em>, in the dark, under fire.</p>

<h2><span class="bk">BOOK VI</span>The Phantom in Our Own Uniform</h2>
<p>Every campaign has its enemy. Ours wore our own colors.</p>
<p>The crews called it the <em>Signed-Out Phantom</em>, and it struck five times. It worked by absence: a probe would question the deep through the common door — the client, scoped by row-level wards — and the deep, seeing no credential, would answer with <em>nothing</em>. Zero rows. And zero looks exactly like privacy. Exactly like emptiness. Exactly like whatever the probe hoped to prove. The first security audit came back all-green — every vault sealed, every secret safe — and it was a lie made entirely of silence. The probe had been standing outside the fortress the whole time, mistaking the locked door for an empty building.</p>
<p>Three versions of that audit fell before the counter-doctrine was nailed to the mast: <em>never trust a scoped read; hard-gate on the credential; and when truth matters, ask the Worker</em> — the one being in the underworld that sees every hall unwarded, and cannot be fooled by absence. The probes reforged on that doctrine came back with the true map at last: one lone argument haunting Caesar's courtroom, zero endorsements, thirteen cases, a pool untouched by any living hand.</p>
<p>The Phantom had kin, and their banners were taken and catalogued like trophies: the <em>Header Ghost</em>, which hid a CORS pennant from every spyglass while a thousand successful crossings proved the strait was open — <em>headers lie; behavior doesn't</em>. The <em>Uniform Trap</em>, which broke a probe on the one vault keyed without an id. And the <em>Guessed Name</em> — the chronicler's own sin — sixteen waves of bombardment hurled for four full minutes against a fortress called <em>amenti-admin</em> that had never existed, while the true fort, named simply <em>Admin</em>, stood unshelled a cable's length away. <em>Identify targets from evidence, never from inference.</em> The lesson cost four minutes and a measure of pride, and was worth a fleet.</p>

<h2><span class="bk">BOOK VII</span>Forging the Keys</h2>
<p>You do not steal keys to gates like these. You forge them — from the metal of the halls themselves.</p>
<p><em>The first key was the Seal</em> — the key to Amenti, the machinery that ends a trial. Its blank was cut from live schema: a verdicts table with a rivet of iron law through its heart, <em>one verdict per charge per week</em>, so that no bell, however many times it rang, could ever double-judge a soul. But the forging exposed the deep secret of the hall: the arguments knew the <em>charge</em> but not the <em>defendant</em>. The name — "Julius Caesar," the name itself — lived only in the Worker's memory. No key cut of pure SQL could ever open this gate. So the Seal was forged <em>into the Worker</em>, thirty lines threaded into a living engine without disturbing a byte of the treasury beating beside it.</p>
<p><em>The second key was the God-View</em> — the key to Hades, hall of the ledger. Not a new power; the probes had proven the Worker always saw everything. The key merely compelled it, for the first time in an eon, to <em>show its operator what it sees</em>: seven vaults read by their true names, minted and burned and circulating counted, the whole underworld in a single answer, behind a single guarded door.</p>
<p><em>The third key was the Glass</em> — the key to Valhalla, hall of the record. One dark pane, emerald and gold, pulse-cards burning like sconces, and along its foot a self-audit strip that cross-examines the god-view against the public halls on every refresh: an instrument sworn to accuse itself before it will lie to its captain. The probe doctrine, hammered into furniture.</p>

<h2><span class="bk">BOOK VIII</span>The Siege of the Glass</h2>
<p>Hosting the Glass became its own battle, fought on fog-bound ground. A private fortress was raised to hold it — and showed the besiegers nothing but a README, blank as a white flag that surrenders nothing. Sixteen waves broke against the wrong name. The Pages watch reported no fires lit. Then the captain played the card only captains hold — <em>Pro</em> — and the fog tore: private fortresses <em>can</em> fly public colors on that commission; the switch had merely never been thrown. It was thrown. The wire from GitHub read like a signal lamp across the harbor: <em>Your site is live.</em></p>
<p>And the final audit brought home the sweetest prize of the war — the retroactive kind, the mystery solved backward. The Glass had been committed under the name <em>index</em>, and so it flew at the fortress <em>root</em>. The README siege, the 404 barrages, the whole confused battle in the fog — all of it explained by a single filename, found by a single probe. Every drift-marker matched the authored pane. The fortress read as a stranger's 404 — <em>truly private while publicly serving</em>. Private source. Public door. Secret-gated blood.</p>
<p>And then, at the last, the captain came to the glass door itself — and a figure stood at the threshold to meet him.</p>
<p>It was <em>Anubis</em>, keeper of the scale, and he asked, very politely, the oldest question of the underworld: <em>present your credentials.</em> All heaven and all hell waited on the answer. The captain was weary now, weary past telling — three keys held at once is a weight no living hand is built for, and some dark storage-force seemed to be drawing the very soul out of him where he stood, as if the halls themselves were feeding on the effort of holding them open. Yet this was the hour of triumph. Millions of compute-cycles, thousands of lines of code, seventy-two hours of siege had led to this one lit door. The gateway had begun to crumble and disintegrate around him; the halls of Amenti shook and convulsed; <em>present your credentials</em> rang and rang in his ears — the moment of truth. He reached for his private keys. Anubis's eyes went wide with anticipation.</p>
<p>And the captain knew exactly what to do. <em>He turned, and walked away from the terminal gate</em> — and it locked behind him, for eons.</p>
<p>He returned to his ship. He opened a quiet channel to the Cloudflare mint-worker. He stood a moment thinking, then crossed to a nearby shelf, drew down a book, turned its pages — and with a smile, put it back. He had chosen. He deposited a <em>single name</em> into the password credentials of the mint-worker, sealed the encryptor, and fired one shot. In seconds the key and its encrypted payload streaked to the satellite gateway high above the earth, and after a moment was buried deep behind the Cloudflare firewall — a wall of fire — never to be seen by anyone again. The master key, the deepest credential in the realm, would not be handed to any doorman, mortal or divine. It would be retired below, where no browser and no god could reach it.</p>
<p>That was the true last skirmish, and its plain meaning is the quietest lesson of the war: the gate had been demanding the <em>master key itself</em>, to be pasted into a browser like handing the crown to a doorman. The captain refused. He rebuilt the ward instead — a humble password for a humble door, trimmed of the whitespace demon that had once stopped him cold at his own threshold — and sent the master key up into the cloud. Which is the great deception of this age, for the cloud is not in the sky at all. It sleeps in the ground: a titanium vault in a fortress of server-farms, ringed by earthwork and razor-wire, fed by satellite uplinks, held under the sovereignty of nations, patrolled overhead by joint strike fighters and guarded past the horizon by whole carrier groups at sea. <em>“Pie in the sky,”</em> the captain would say, and smile — for he alone knew the joke: no safer place on Earth than the sky. Only then did Captain Ingram stand before his own gate, on his own ship, under a sky gone finally quiet, and type that humble password into a dark field, and press the words <em>OPEN THE GLASS</em> —</p>
<div class="gate">— A N D&nbsp;&nbsp;S A W&nbsp;&nbsp;T H E&nbsp;&nbsp;L I G H T —</div>
<p>Pulse-cards igniting one by one down the pane like torches down a hall: <em>one user. One argument. Zero endorsements. Four hundred eighty minted. Zero burned. Zero verdicts.</em> Seventy-two hours of war, rendered as eight small numbers glowing emerald against the dark. The gates of Amenti, Valhalla, and Hades stood open, and the captain stood in all three halls at once, holding all three keys, looking at everything.</p>

<h2><span class="bk">BOOK IX</span>The Twist at the Gates</h2>
<p>The chronicle must end with what the last probes found, because it is the strangest thing in the record.</p>
<p>The final sweep went down to inventory the conquered halls — and came back reporting that the docket, the great schedule of trials nailed to the courthouse door, was <em>already full</em>. Thirteen cases, shining, listed, arraigned. No one had posted them. No seeding party had gone down. The army checked its own logs twice.</p>
<p>The truth, when it surfaced, rearranged the meaning of the entire war: <em>the docket seeds itself.</em> The cases were never data waiting to be planted — they were <em>code</em>, cut into the Worker's own body when the engine was forged, and the docket is only a window onto that living stone. It populates the moment anyone looks at it. It always will. The besiegers had fought three days to open gates onto halls that had been, in this one uncanny sense, announcing themselves the whole time — thirteen trials proclaimed on a door an eon locked, waiting not for a key but for an audience.</p>
<p>But behind each proclaimed door: an empty courtroom. And one ghost — Caesar's orphan argument, written by the captain's own hand in the war's first hour, the sole living thing on the stage. And the Seal, newly forged and dutiful, set to wake at the very next bell and perform its first autonomous act: to solemnly judge that ghost, and rule <em>HUNG</em> on a trial that never happened, unless the captain swept the stage before midnight.</p>
<p>So the war ends the way true wars end — won, and unfinished. The gates stand open. The three keys are set down at last — surrendered, sent below, no longer a weight on any living hand. The docket proclaims its thirteen trials to a harbor that doesn't yet know to listen. And somewhere ashore is a second human being who will one day sign up cold, read two arguments about a dead king, and spend five emeralds on a conviction — the single act, every probe agreed, on which the whole underworld turns over and begins.</p>
<p>The Flying Dutchman rides her anchor in the quiet after the fireworks. The Glass glows in a dark cabin, all zeroes and one ghost. The court convenes on the thirteenth.</p>
<p>And in the last moment of the record, the Giant Pyramid begins to collapse — hall folding into hall, an eon of locked stone coming down in silence behind the fleeing ship. The captain had won; the battle was over. The Flying Dutchman raced for the horizon.</p>
<p>He was weary now — not the sharp weariness of the siege, but the long, loosened kind that comes only after a great weight is finally set down. The keys were gone from him. Whatever they had been drawing out of him all those hours had stopped drawing. At the console beside the flight controls he set an unknown course into the flight computer — no heading he could have named, only <em>away, and onward</em> — and pressed <em>enter</em>. He glanced once at the quiet sky, where his master key slept in its buried vault. <em>“Pie in the sky,”</em> he murmured, to no one, and let it go.</p>
<p>Then, unhurried, he made himself a drink. The captain was fond of seltzer water and orange juice, and he hand-mixed it the way he liked: rare carbonated water drawn from deep within the earth, and fresh oranges from the captain's own pantry. He pulled an iPad from the shelf and scrolled his book collection, and began to read — a few lines of text, no more. He smiled, and drank the cool, refreshing elixir.</p>
<p>And as he read on, a thought occurred to him. Luckily, the console sat within reach; he wrote it down quickly, before it could slip away — and the ship sailed on into the dark, carrying the captain and his one small note toward whatever the next war would be.</p>
<div class="gate">— T H E&nbsp;&nbsp;E N D —</div>

<hr>
<p style="text-align:center;font-family:var(--font-elite);opacity:.8">Here ends the Chronicle of the Siege. The technical record of the campaign — doctrine, diagrams, and the full probe manifest — follows below, in the field journal <em>“Keeping Your Powder Dry.”</em></p>
<div class="finis">INGRAM MANOR LLC · THE FLYING DUTCHMAN · NEW YORK HARBOR · JULY 4–7, 2026 ◈</div>
</div>

</main>

<hr class="section-rule">

<section class="brief-body">

<div class="wrap">

<div class="masthead">
  <div class="eyebrow">Field Engineering Journal · Final Entry · Capstone</div>
  <h1>KEEPING YOUR POWDER DRY</h1>
  <div class="subtitle">We spent all our resources on bots. The bots leveraged our power — and kept the powder dry. The complete technical debrief of the Fourth of July campaign: doctrine, architecture, the six false-verdict artifact species, the keys as shipped code, the campaign in numbers — and the self-seeding twist that rewrote the ending.</div>
  
  <div class="byline">Ingram Manor LLC · Captain Ian Ingram · Aboard the Flying Dutchman, New York Harbor · July 4–7, 2026</div>
</div>



<h2 class="s-doct">0 · The Doctrine — why "powder dry" means "send bots"</h2>
<p>The campaign's entire resource budget went into one thing: <em>probes</em> — read-only, self-downloading console scripts, each syntax-checked before deployment, each expendable. The human touched code as rarely as possible. This is not laziness; it is leverage. A human inspecting a system reads what they came to read. A probe built to the doctrine returns the datum <em>and</em> everything adjacent to it — and the adjacent findings decided this campaign at every turn.</p>
<div class="callout">DOCTRINE: A probe must return ~100× the datum requested. The finding you were not looking for is the finding. Dry powder = human effort never spent where a script could be spent instead.</div>
<p>The canonical probe skeleton, as deployed forty-plus times:</p>
<pre><span class="c">/* read-only · self-downloading · fingerprints its own target */</span>
(<span class="k">async</span> () =&gt; {
  <span class="k">var</span> B=[], L=(...a)=&gt;{B.push(a.join(<span class="s">' '</span>)); console.log(...a);};
  <span class="c">// 1. FINGERPRINT — identify where you are from evidence, not inference</span>
  <span class="c">// 2. READ through the layer that cannot lie (service-key endpoints)</span>
  <span class="c">// 3. RETURN more than asked — sweep the neighbors of the question</span>
  <span class="c">// 4. VERDICT — one line naming the next action</span>
  <span class="k">var</span> b=<span class="k">new</span> Blob([B.join(<span class="s">'\n'</span>)],{type:<span class="s">'text/plain'</span>});
  <span class="c">// anchor-click download → the report leaves the battlefield with you</span>
})();</pre>

<h2 class="s-terr">1 · The Terrain — two-layer architecture (and why it matters)</h2>
<div class="fig">
<div class="figcap">Fig. 1 · The two layers: scoped users, unscoped system</div>
<svg viewBox="0 0 840 300" width="100%">
<rect x="20" y="20" width="380" height="110" fill="none" stroke="#3a3a52"/>
<text x="35" y="45" fill="#00ffe0" font-size="16">LAYER 1 · CLIENT (RLS-scoped)</text>
<text x="35" y="70" fill="#c8c8d8" font-size="15">users → supabase client → own rows only</text>
<text x="35" y="90" fill="#8888a0" font-size="14">signed out ⇒ ZERO ROWS (the Phantom lives here)</text>
<text x="35" y="112" fill="#e03030" font-size="14">4 probes fooled · never audit security from here</text>
<rect x="440" y="20" width="380" height="110" fill="none" stroke="#d4a017"/>
<text x="455" y="45" fill="#d4a017" font-size="16">LAYER 2 · WORKER (service key, unscoped)</text>
<text x="455" y="70" fill="#c8c8d8" font-size="15">feeds · pool · settle · seal · /admin/overview</text>
<text x="455" y="90" fill="#8888a0" font-size="14">sees everything, always — immune to the Phantom</text>
<text x="455" y="112" fill="#39d98a" font-size="14">every reliable finding of the war came through here</text>
<rect x="230" y="180" width="380" height="90" fill="none" stroke="#00ffe0"/>
<text x="245" y="205" fill="#00ffe0" font-size="16">SUPABASE · 7 tables (heterogeneous keys!)</text>
<text x="245" y="228" fill="#c8c8d8" font-size="14">arguments · votes · reports · ledger · balance(user_id!) · profiles · verdicts</text>
<text x="245" y="250" fill="#8888a0" font-size="14">emerald_balance has NO id column — the Uniform Trap</text>
<line x1="210" y1="130" x2="360" y2="180" stroke="#3a3a52"/>
<line x1="630" y1="130" x2="480" y2="180" stroke="#d4a017"/>
</svg>
</div>
<p>The single most consequential fact of the terrain: an admin god-view is <em>not</em> a privilege added to a user — it is layer 2 finally reporting to its operator. The Worker always saw everything (it settles pools; it must). <code style="font-family:var(--font-mono);font-size:16px">/admin/overview</code> was the third use of an <code style="font-family:var(--font-mono);font-size:16px">x-admin-secret</code> guard already live on settle and seal. Pattern completion, not premise violation.</p>

<h2 class="s-enemy">2 · The Enemy — six species of false verdict (the artifact catalog)</h2>
<table>
<tr><th>#</th><th>Species</th><th>Mechanism</th><th>Strikes</th><th>Standing order</th></tr>
<tr><td>1</td><td>Signed-Out Phantom</td><td>RLS returns 0 rows to no-session; zero reads as "private"/"empty"</td><td class="bad">5</td><td>Hard-gate on session; read truth via layer 2</td></tr>
<tr><td>2</td><td>Header Ghost</td><td>CORS headers consumed by browser, invisible to JS → false "no CORS"</td><td class="bad">1</td><td>Test behavior, never headers</td></tr>
<tr><td>3</td><td>Uniform Trap</td><td>select('id') on a table keyed user_id</td><td class="bad">1</td><td>Read each table by its real key</td></tr>
<tr><td>4</td><td>Guessed Name</td><td>16 polls × 15s against repo "amenti-admin"; real name: "Admin"</td><td class="bad">1 (4 min)</td><td>Targets from evidence, never inference</td></tr>
<tr><td>5</td><td>Origin-without-Path</td><td>probe logged origin, not pathname — findings attributed to nothing</td><td class="bad">1</td><td>Fingerprint the page you stand on</td></tr>
<tr><td>6</td><td>Placeholder Count</td><td>"— none —" filler row counted as data (verdicts "1 row")</td><td class="bad">1</td><td>Count content, not markup</td></tr>
</table>
<p>Note the shape: every species is a <em>true raw datum</em> supporting a <em>false conclusion</em>. The probes never lied; the interpretations did. The counter-doctrine that ended the Phantom's run is the campaign's chief export:</p>
<div class="callout">When the question is "what is actually in the system," ask the Worker — the layer that cannot be fooled by absence. Scoped reads answer "what can I see," which is a different question.</div>

<h2 class="s-keys">3 · The Keys — as shipped code</h2>
<p><span class="key k1">Key I — THE SEAL</span> <span class="gate">(gate of Amenti)</span>. The gotcha that decided its home: <code style="font-family:var(--font-mono);font-size:16px">arguments</code> stores <code style="font-family:var(--font-mono);font-size:16px">topic_id</code> but the <em>figure</em> lives only in the Worker's TOPICS map. Pure SQL cannot name a defendant ⇒ the seal runs in the Worker. Core:</p>
<pre><span class="fn">async function</span> sealCharges(env, weekOf) {
  <span class="c">// read every live (visible) argument's pan + votes — service key</span>
  <span class="c">// sum per (topic, pan) → verdict = leading pan, or 'HUNG' at 0/tie</span>
  <span class="k">const</span> figure = TOPICS[topicId].facets.figure[0];  <span class="c">// only the Worker knows this</span>
  <span class="c">// INSERT verdict … Prefer: resolution=ignore-duplicates   ← idempotency rivet</span>
  <span class="c">// UNIQUE(topic_id, week_of) ⇒ a re-fired cron CANNOT double-seal</span>
  <span class="c">// PATCH arguments status: visible → sealed                ← the freeze</span>
}</pre>
<p><span class="key k2">Key II — THE GOD-VIEW</span> <span class="gate">(gate of Hades)</span>. Seven service-key reads, each by its table's <em>true</em> key (the Uniform Trap made structural), plus rollups: minted/burned/circulating, open cases, pending reports, verdicts sealed. One read, whole platform.</p>
<p><span class="key k3">Key III — THE GLASS</span> <span class="gate">(gate of Valhalla)</span>. One pane of HTML; secret in sessionStorage only; and the doctrine as furniture — a self-audit strip that cross-examines the god-view against public endpoints on every refresh:</p>
<pre><span class="c">// the instrument accuses itself before it will lie to its operator</span>
pool.totalVotes === admin.argument_votes.count ? <span class="s">'agree'</span> : <span class="s">'DRIFT'</span>  <span class="c">// red</span></pre>
<p>The gate itself was re-armed mid-campaign: five forged credentials, five 403s (strict, no backdoor) — then a second ward added so the master key retires to Cloudflare forever:</p>
<pre><span class="fn">function</span> isAdmin(request, env) {
  <span class="k">const</span> cred = (request.headers.get(<span class="s">'x-admin-secret'</span>) || <span class="s">''</span>).trim();  <span class="c">// ← kills the whitespace demon</span>
  <span class="k">return</span> !!cred && (cred === env.SUPABASE_SECRET || cred === env.ADMIN_PASSWORD);
}</pre>

<h2 class="s-camp">4 · The Campaign in Numbers</h2>
<div class="fig">
<div class="figcap">Fig. 4 · The Cognitive Multiplier — how 10 problems became thousands of verified answers before the Glass ever lit</div>
<svg viewBox="0 0 860 560" width="100%">
<!-- LEFT PANEL: the cascade -->
<text x="30" y="34" fill="#d4a017" font-size="17" font-weight="bold">THE CASCADE</text>

<text x="46" y="72" fill="#e03030" font-size="15">10 MAIN PROBLEMS</text>
<text x="46" y="90" fill="#8888a0" font-size="15">seed · seal · rotation · bell · auth · glass · gates…</text>
<line x1="180" y1="98" x2="180" y2="122" stroke="#3a3a52"/><text x="192" y="116" fill="#8888a0" font-size="15">× ~4 each</text>

<text x="46" y="142" fill="#e8c766" font-size="15">40+ PROBES DEPLOYED</text>
<text x="46" y="160" fill="#8888a0" font-size="15">read-only · self-downloading · expendable</text>
<line x1="180" y1="168" x2="180" y2="192" stroke="#3a3a52"/><text x="192" y="186" fill="#8888a0" font-size="15">× ~8 each</text>
<rect x="30" y="192" width="300" height="46" fill="none" stroke="#00ffe0"/>
<text x="46" y="212" fill="#00ffe0" font-size="15">100s OF QUESTIONS to LIVE CODE</text>
<text x="46" y="230" fill="#8888a0" font-size="15">every pan · every table · every gate · every origin</text>
<line x1="180" y1="238" x2="180" y2="262" stroke="#3a3a52"/><text x="192" y="256" fill="#8888a0" font-size="15">× ~10 each</text>
<rect x="30" y="262" width="300" height="46" fill="none" stroke="#39d98a"/>
<text x="46" y="282" fill="#39d98a" font-size="15">1000s OF VERIFIED ANSWERS</text>
